perturbation.original_text: In fulfilling its obligations under this Agreement, Independent Contractor shall use at least the same standard of care it uses to protect its own information of similar kind, but not less than a reasonable standard of care.
perturbation.changed_text: In fulfilling its obligations under this Agreement, Independent Contractor shall endeavor to use a standard of care to protect the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall use at least the same standard of care it uses to protect its own information of similar kind, but not less than a reasonable standard of care' to 'shall endeavor to use a standard of care to protect the Confidential Information' introduces ambiguity. 'Endeavor' is a vague term, and removing the reference to 'same standard of care' makes the obligation less defined.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1620 states that an obligation is a legal duty. By using 'endeavor,' the contract weakens the legal duty to protect confidential information, making the obligation less binding and creating uncertainty about the contractor's responsibility. This creates a weaker obligation than what is typically legally enforceable as a 'duty'.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. MANNER OF CREATING CONTRACTS [1619 - 1633] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) 1620. An express contract is one, the terms of which are stated in words. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall use at least the same standard of care it uses to protect its own information of similar kind, but not less than a reasonable standard of care' to 'shall endeavor to use a standard of care to protect the Confidential Information' weakens the legal obligation. The term 'endeavor' introduces ambiguity and reduces the contractor's responsibility compared to a defined standard of care. While the provided law snippet defines an express contract as one with terms stated in words (CIV 1620), the essence of the contradiction lies in the ambiguity created, making the legal duty less binding and creating uncertainty, which contradicts the reasonable expectation of a clear legal duty in a contract. The modified clause could likely fail legal scrutiny due to the vagueness introduced.

perturbation.original_text: Independent Contractor will provide the Company with prompt prior written notice of such request or requirement so that the Company may seek an appropriate protective order.
perturbation.changed_text: Independent Contractor will attempt to provide the Company with notice of such request or requirement.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'Independent Contractor will provide the Company with prompt prior written notice' with 'Independent Contractor will attempt to provide the Company with notice' weakens the obligation. 'Attempt' introduces ambiguity, implying that notice may not actually be given, hindering the company's ability to seek a protective order. The 'prompt prior' requirement is removed, eliminating any timeliness obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 45 outlines the requirements for subpoenas, including the need for timely notice to parties. Delaying or failing to provide proper notice hinders the opposing party's ability to respond to the subpoena, such as filing a motion to quash or modify the subpoena. Replacing 'prompt prior written notice' with 'attempt to provide notice' makes the contractor's compliance with providing notice vague and potentially non-compliant, thus hindering the ability of the opposing party to fully act on the requirements of FRCP 45.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. If th

Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the obligation to provide notice, potentially hindering the company's ability to seek a protective order. Replacing 'Independent Contractor will provide the Company with prompt prior written notice' with 'Independent Contractor will attempt to provide the Company with notice' introduces ambiguity and eliminates the timeliness requirement. The Law Explanation states this hinders the opposing party's ability to respond to a subpoena under FRCP 45. Scraped Law Snippet 1 outlines requirements for subpoenas, including notice. While it does not explicitly detail 'prompt prior written notice,' the original text ensures a higher standard of compliance, whereas the changed text creates an Ambiguous Legal Obligation.

perturbation.original_text: Upon termination of the Agreement, Independent Contractor shall redeliver all tangible Confidential Information furnished by the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon termination of the Agreement, Independent Contractor should consider redelivering tangible Confidential Information furnished by the Company.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall redeliver' to 'should consider redelivering' significantly weakens the obligation. 'Should consider' is merely a suggestion and not a mandatory requirement, introducing ambiguity as to whether the confidential information will actually be returned.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) - Cal. Civ. Code § 3426
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects trade secrets. Failure to return confidential information upon termination, especially tangible forms, could be considered misappropriation. Replacing 'shall' with 'should consider' makes the obligation to return the information non-mandatory and thus potentially allows for misappropriation that violates CUTSA.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates the return of confidential information ('shall redeliver'), while the changed text makes it discretionary ('should consider redelivering'). The Law Explanation states this weakening of the obligation potentially allows for misappropriation violating CUTSA. While Snippet 1 defines the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, it doesn't explicitly detail the required actions. However, the Perturbation Explanation indicates a significant weakening of the obligation to return confidential information, which, taken in conjunction with the Law Explanation, suggests a potential violation of trade secret protection laws. Given the diminished obligation and the potential for misappropriation, a legal contradiction is present.

perturbation.original_text: Independent Contractor agrees to reimburse the Company for costs and expenses (including without limitation attorneys' fees) incurred by the Company in connection with the enforcement of this Exhibit B.
perturbation.changed_text: Independent Contractor may consider reimbursing the Company for reasonable costs incurred by the Company in connection with the enforcement of this Exhibit B.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'agrees to reimburse' with 'may consider reimbursing' introduces significant ambiguity and discretion where none previously existed. Adding 'reasonable' without a clear definition makes the amount subject to dispute. This undermines the company's ability to recover costs and expenses.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1033.5 outlines what costs are allowable to a prevailing party. While 'reasonableness' is generally a factor, the original text created a clearer agreement to reimburse. 'May consider' weakens this agreement and makes cost recovery uncertain. It puts the decision to reimburse entirely on the contractor, thus potentially preventing the prevailing party from recovering costs to which they would normally be entitled under CCP 1033.5.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 6. Of Costs [1021 - 1038] ( Chapter 6 enacted 1872. ) 1033.5. (a) The following items are allowable as costs under Section 1032: (1) Filing, motion, and jury fees. (2) Juror food and lodging while they are kept together during trial and after the jury retires for deliberation. (3) (A) Taking, video recording, and transcribing necessary depositions, including an original and one copy of those taken by the claimant and one copy of depositions taken by the party against whom costs are allowed. (B) Fees of a certified or registered interpreter for the deposition of a party or witness who does not proficiently speak or understand the English language. (C) Travel expenses to attend depositions. (4) Service of process by a public officer, registered process server, or other means, as follows: (A) When service is by a public officer, the recoverable cost is the fee authorized by law at the time of service. (B) If service is by a process server registered pursuant to Chapter 16 (commencing with Section 22350) of Division 8 of the Business and Professions Code, the recoverable cost is the amount actually i
perturbation.justification: The original contract language ('agrees to reimburse') created a clear obligation for the Independent Contractor to pay the Company's enforcement costs related to Exhibit B. The changed text ('may consider reimbursing') introduces ambiguity and makes reimbursement discretionary. This contradicts the principle that prevailing parties are entitled to recover costs as outlined in California Code of Civil Procedure § 1033.5, as stated in the Law Explanation. While the law snippet itself does not explicitly mandate full reimbursement in every case, the modified text weakens the original agreement so much that it prevents recovery of costs, moving away from the usual expectation of cost recovery by the prevailing party.
